params.text:
Spotting dehydration

Dehydration often creeps up quietly, so it helps to know the signs. Watching urine is one of the simplest checks: pale urine usually means someone is well hydrated, while dark, strong-smelling urine is an early warning that they need more to drink.

  • Early signs: dark urine, dry mouth and lips, thirst, headache.
  • Feeling unwell: tiredness, dizziness, constipation.
  • Serious signs: confusion, sunken eyes, drowsiness, few wet trips to the toilet.

Why dehydration is dangerous

Dehydration is not a minor problem. It can trigger urinary and chest infections, constipation, pressure ulcers, falls and sudden confusion, and it can make existing conditions much worse. Severe dehydration can lead to kidney problems and hospital admission.

In older people the warning is that confusion, drowsiness or an unexplained fall can be the first sign of dehydration, so never dismiss these changes.

params.question: Which of these is an early sign that someone may be becoming dehydrated?

answers.text: Dark, strong-smelling urine together with a dry mouth and thirst.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Yes. Dark urine and a dry mouth are common early warnings that someone needs more to drink.

answers.text: Pale, plentiful urine and clear, moist lips.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: These are signs of good hydration, not dehydration.

answers.text: Feeling too full after a large drink.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This is not a sign of dehydration.

answers.text: Sleeping well and having plenty of energy.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Dehydration tends to cause tiredness and low energy, not the opposite.
